figures.gloss: A supreme creator deity made of intertwined noodles, two meatballs, and googly eyes. According to Pastafarian dogma, He deliberately created a flawed universe after heavy drinking and actively alters scientific measurements with His Noodly Appendage to test faith.
figures.facet: It satirizes intelligent design by fulfilling all its structural requirements while aggressively stripping away its unstated anthropomorphic and Abrahamic dignity, proving that structural validity does not equal theological truth.
figures.provenance: Bobby Henderson's open letter to the Kansas State Board of Education (2005).

figures.gloss: The classical goddess of chaos, resurrected as the central deity of Discordianism. She represents the fundamental reality of disorder, playfully or maliciously subverting human attempts to impose artificial grids of rules, laws, and logic upon the universe.
figures.facet: Unlike her classical counterpart who merely incites petty feuds, this Eris is elevated to the supreme cosmological truth: chaos is not the absence of order, but the primal, creative, and necessary ground of all being.
figures.provenance: 'Principia Discordia' by Greg Hill and Kerry Wendell Thornley (1963).

figures.gloss: The pipe-smoking, endlessly grinning figurehead and prophet of the Church of the SubGenius. A 1950s clip-art salesman endowed with supreme financial and spiritual acumen, he promises his followers salvation from the mundane conspiracy of normal life.
figures.facet: He merges the savior archetype with the mid-century capitalist grifter. Salvation is not achieved through moral purity, but by paying financial dues and embracing radical, absurd self-interest under his smiling gaze.
figures.provenance: Foundation pamphlet of the Church of the SubGenius (1979) by Ivan Stang and Philo Drummond.

figures.gloss: The holy grail of the SubGenius, an ineffable state of effortless success, freedom, and independence. It is the natural, joyful birthright of mutants, perpetually stolen by the Conspiracy of 'Normals' and requiring constant absurdity to reclaim.
figures.facet: It commodifies nirvana into a defense against late capitalism. Instead of enlightenment through discipline, it demands enlightenment through aggressive laziness, surrealism, and the avoidance of conventional labor.
figures.provenance: Church of the SubGenius literature (1979); heavily influenced by 1970s counterculture.

figures.gloss: An omnipresent energy field created by all living things, which surrounds, penetrates, and binds the galaxy together. In its real-world religious adoption, it serves as a nondenominational, pantheistic spiritual energy that practitioners seek to align with.
figures.facet: It translates cinematic worldbuilding into workable modern theology. It provides the mystical comfort of Eastern dualistic monism but is culturally mediated through American pop-sci-fi, requiring no adherence to actual Asian heritage or asceticism.
figures.provenance: George Lucas's 'Star Wars' (1977); adopted by modern Jediism movements like the Temple of the Jedi Order (2001).

figures.gloss: The cinematic slacker elevated to a modern sage in Dudeism. Clad in a bathrobe and drinking White Russians, he navigates the chaos of modern aggression through passive non-resistance, 'abiding' when confronted with absurdity.
figures.facet: The prophet as holy burnout. He achieves harmony with the universe not through rigorous asceticism or agonizing spiritual searching, but through a profound, principled apathy toward ambition, status, and societal expectations.
figures.provenance: The Coen Brothers' film 'The Big Lebowski' (1998); systematized by Oliver Benjamin in 'The Church of the Latter-Day Dude' (2005).

figures.gloss: The sacred act of digital duplication. Adherents of Kopimism view the keyboard shortcuts Ctrl-C and Ctrl-V as holy rites, asserting that all information possesses inherent value and that multiplying it is a supreme moral imperative.
figures.facet: It sacralizes copyright infringement. Where traditional religions meticulously preserve fixed, unalterable sacred texts, this tradition insists the sheer act of digital duplication itself—regardless of content—is the highest expression of the divine will.
figures.provenance: Founded by Isak Gerson in Sweden (2010) as a religious defense of file-sharing.

figures.gloss: A paradoxical goddess who is simultaneously perfectly invisible and entirely pink. She delights in stealing socks from the laundry and is invoked primarily by skeptics to highlight the logical pitfalls of demanding faith in unfalsifiable divine properties.
figures.facet: The deity as a pure logical trap. She is specifically engineered to weaponize cognitive dissonance, proving that human minds can venerate literally contradictory attributes if they are cloaked in the reverent syntax of theology.

figures.gloss: Bertrand Russell's 1952 hypothetical: a china teapot too small to be seen by any telescope, orbiting the sun somewhere between Earth and Mars. Russell used it to argue that the burden of proof for an unfalsifiable claim rests on the person asserting it, not on the skeptic obliged to disprove it.
figures.facet: It is the tradition's founding logical axiom rather than a deity — the argument that the FSM and the Invisible Pink Unicorn exist specifically to dramatize and popularize.
figures.provenance: Bertrand Russell, 'Is There a God?', commissioned by Illustrated magazine (1952), first published in Russell's Collected Papers (1997).

figures.gloss: Discordianism's central emblem: a yin-yang-style disc split into the Hodge (a pentagon, representing order) and the Podge (representing disorder), topped by the golden apple of Kallisti inscribed 'to the fairest.' It is printed and explained in the Principia Discordia as the tradition's core icon.
figures.facet: It borrows the yin-yang's visual balance only to subvert its claim of harmony — insisting order and disorder are equally illusory human overlays on a formless, prior chaos, rather than two halves of one true whole.
figures.provenance: Principia Discordia (Greg Hill & Kerry Wendell Thornley, 1963 ed.).

figures.gloss: The pen-name and 'Hierophant' persona of Greg Hill, co-author with Kerry Thornley (writing as Omar Khayyam Ravenhurst) of the Principia Discordia (1963) — the text that presents Malaclypse as Discordianism's founding prophet.
figures.facet: A prophet who advertises his own fabrication in the same breath as his revelation, collapsing the usual distance between a movement's founder and its acknowledged fiction.
figures.provenance: Principia Discordia (1963), attributed throughout to 'Malaclypse the Younger, Hierophant.'

figures.gloss: Diego Armando Maradona deified as 'D10S' — a portmanteau of Dios (God) and his shirt number 10 — by the Iglesia Maradoniana, founded in Rosario, Argentina, in 1998. Adherents count years from his birth (30 October 1960 = Year 1) and recite a creed adapted from the Lord's Prayer; his handball goal against England in the 1986 World Cup, publicly admitted as a foul, is treated as a founding miracle ('the Hand of God').
figures.facet: The one figure here worshipped as a literal, if playful, god by a real, continuously operating congregation with its own calendar — and the roster's only entry from outside the US/North-Atlantic internet axis that produced most of the others.
figures.provenance: Iglesia Maradoniana, founded Rosario, Argentina (1998); widely reported at Maradona's death (25 November 2020).

figures.gloss: The Discordian guerrilla-absurdist campaign of hoaxes, forged flyers, planted rumors, and deliberately self-contradicting claims, associated with Kerry Thornley and popularized through Robert Anton Wilson and Robert Shea's The Illuminatus! Trilogy (1975), aimed at destabilizing any single confident account of reality.
figures.facet: Discordianism's closest thing to a practice: not worship but the manufacture of confusion, treating epistemic destabilization itself as the sacrament.
figures.provenance: Robert Anton Wilson & Robert Shea, The Illuminatus! Trilogy (1975); Discordian Society ephemera and correspondence, 1960s–70s.

figures.gloss: July 5, 1998 — the date Church of the SubGenius founder Ivan Stang prophesied that alien 'X-ists' would arrive to destroy the Earth and rapture paid-up members onto 'Pleasure Saucers.' When the world failed to end, the date was reinterpreted rather than abandoned, and it is now marked annually as a mock-apocalyptic gathering.
figures.facet: A prophecy engineered to fail on a fixed schedule and then absorbed into doctrine instead of refuted by it, turning the psychology of failed apocalypse into a recurring holiday rather than a crisis.
figures.provenance: Church of the SubGenius literature (Ivan Stang, prophecy dated from the early 1990s); annual X-Day gatherings, 1998–present.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The internet's twin satirical deities designed specifically to mock unfalsifiable intelligent design and theological paradox.
Intra-tradition bond: 'Bob' is the ultimate avatar and dispenser of Slack, holding the monopoly on its distribution to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The patron of chaos and discord who upends the artificial rules constructed by the ruling pantheon to inject necessary unpredictability.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Discordian concept of Aneristic (order) and Eristic (chaos) illusions directly mirrors the dualistic interplay of Yin and Yang, though privileging chaos as the base reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The smiling, slick-talking psychopomp of commerce and deception, leading followers through the marketplace of absurdity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The pipe-smoking trickster of the crossroads who mediates between the divine and the profane through commerce, loopholes, and humor.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An ineffable state of natural flow and effortless action (wu wei), framed here through the lens of dodging wage-labor and societal expectation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ultimate path of yielding, non-resistance, and finding harmony by simply rolling with the cosmic gutters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A pervasive, invisible spiritual energy present in all living things that can be tapped by practitioners to influence reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An impersonal, all-encompassing metaphysical reality that binds the universe, which the enlightened mind seeks to merge with harmoniously.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A cosmological structure defined entirely by the perpetual war between equal, inherently opposed Light and Dark sides of a unified substance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The FSM explicitly mimics the Old Testament God's jealousy, creation mechanics, and arbitrary commandments (the 'I'd Really Rather You Didn'ts').
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The demiurgic creator who wills the universe into existence, parodied here as a drunken monster conjuring a visibly flawed world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The utterly transcendent, ineffable supreme being defined entirely by contradictory or apophatic (negative) traits beyond human comprehension.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmic adversary to order, though Discordianism inverts the Zoroastrian valence by making the chaotic destroyer the necessary hero.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The deification of knowledge-recording and replication, upgrading the sacred scribe's pen to the mass-duplication of the digital keyboard.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rebellious fire-bringer, distributing locked, proprietary knowledge to the masses via raw, unapologetic duplication.
Direct adoption of the same invented Discordian goddess — chaos magic imports Eris from the Principia Discordia wholesale rather than developing an independent figure.
Intra-tradition bond: Russell's unfalsifiable orbiting teapot is the direct rhetorical ancestor of the FSM's parody-deity argument.
Intra-tradition bond: both the IPU and the teapot exist to make Russell's burden-of-proof argument vivid rather than to be believed in.
Intra-tradition bond: the Sacred Chao is Eris's own emblem, printed beside her myth in the same founding text (Principia Discordia).
Intra-tradition bond: Malaclypse presents himself as the goddess's founding prophet and scribe in the Principia Discordia.
Intra-tradition bond: Hill and Thornley, authors of the Principia Discordia under their Discordian personas, are also the campaign's originators.
Intra-tradition bond: the guerrilla-hoax campaign is Discordianism's closest thing to a practice performed in the goddess's name.
Intra-tradition bond: X-Day is 'Bob's' own prophesied event, the Church of the SubGenius's central eschatological rite.
Intra-tradition bond: eternal Slack aboard the Pleasure Saucers is the promised reward X-Day's rapture was to deliver.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two contemporary deification cults built around a real, named, publicly documented human — one Argentine football, one Melanesian colonial encounter — each with its own founding narrative and ongoing devotional practice.

dossier.essence: A modern, largely Anglophone and internet-native family of self-consciously invented, satirical, or fiction-derived religions — Discordianism (1960s), the Church of the SubGenius (1979), Jediism, Dudeism, Pastafarianism / the Church of the Flying Spaghetti Monster (2005), and Kopimism (2010) among them. They range from pure literary joke to sincerely practiced community, and are often deployed to satirize religion, test church-state boundaries, or build ironic belonging.
dossier.cosmos: There is no shared cosmology; each borrows or parodies one. Discordianism sacralizes Chaos, personified as the goddess Eris, as the creative ground of being; Pastafarianism answers intelligent-design creationism with a noodly drunken creator; Jediism adopts the Force as an impersonal energy field; Dudeism preaches a Taoist-flavored ease. The shared move is meta-cosmological: that the forms of religion can be occupied playfully, and that deliberately unfalsifiable deities (the Flying Spaghetti Monster, the Invisible Pink Unicorn) expose the logical structure of belief itself.
dossier.practice: Activity spans the joke-to-sincere spectrum — Discordian pranks ('Operation Mindfuck'), SubGenius 'devivals' and the pursuit of 'Slack,' Pastafarian pirate regalia and colander-in-the-license-photo legal claims, Dudeist online ordinations, and census campaigns (the 2001 'Jedi' census phenomenon). Much of it lives online and functions as what scholars call 'hyper-real' or fiction-based religion.
dossier.canon: Founding satirical texts and their pop-culture sources: the 'Principia Discordia' (1963/1965), 'The Book of the SubGenius' (1983), Bobby Henderson's open letter and 'The Gospel of the Flying Spaghetti Monster' (2006), plus films and franchises (Star Wars, 'The Big Lebowski') repurposed as scripture.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds religion turned on itself — the full apparatus of myth, ritual, and creed wielded as satire and boundary-test, showing that the form of the sacred can be inhabited without, or against, belief.
sources: Adam Possamai, 'Religion and Popular Culture: A Hyper-Real Testament' (2005)
Carole M. Cusack, 'Invented Religions: Imagination, Fiction and Faith' (2010)
Markus Altena Davidsen, 'Fiction-based Religion: Conceptualising a New Category against History-based Religion and Fandom' (2013)
